Building on AB 209’s recommendation of an 82°F maximum for living spaces, SB 655 creates a statutory foundation for setting building standards for new construction and retrofit incentives for existing homes and disclosure requirements around cooling ability. If implemented, it could reduce heat exposure for millions of Californians across diverse climate zones.

O-59 represents a model for how cities can redefine housing safety in the face of rising heat.

The initiative is a part of broader adoption of Passive House standards: 48 cities and towns now require Passive House for large new multifamily buildings, with over 200 projects (nearly 6,000 units) in development. Case Study Title: Arizona's Heat Tiers system delivers targeted early warnings based on real-time health data
Description: Traditional heat warnings treat all areas within a forecast zone identically, but health impacts vary significantly based on local conditions and population vulnerability. Arizona's Heat Tiers system uses real-time syndromic surveillance data, tracking emergency department visits for heat-related illness, to assess heat risk by county and assign tiered alert levels that trigger different response activities. Counties receiving a higher tier designation known to open additional cooling centers, surge public communications, and activate welfare check programs. By tying alert levels to actual health outcomes rather than temperature alone, Arizona ensures that emergency resources are deployed where and when they are most needed.

Case Study Title: New Jersey's Code Red policies establish a clear legal framework for heat emergency response
Description: Inconsistent local responses to heat emergencies mean that people in some New Jersey municipalities receive robust protections while others receive little or none. New Jersey's Code Red legislation establishes a statewide framework for declaring and responding to heat emergencies, standardizing the thresholds and response activities that local governments must deploy when dangerous heat is forecast. By creating a baseline legal requirement, New Jersey ensures that every resident can expect their local government to activate cooling centers, conduct outreach to vulnerable populations, and coordinate with state resources during a Code Red event.

Case Study Title: California's emergency load reduction programs help prevent grid failure during extreme heat waves
Description: When extreme heat drives electricity demand to record levels, the risk of grid failure rises. California's Emergency Load Reduction Program incentivizes large electricity users to voluntarily reduce their consumption during grid emergencies, helping shave peak demand and prevent cascading outages. The program provides financial incentives for customers who curtail usage when called upon, turning large electricity users into grid stabilizers rather than demand spikes. California's program demonstrates that demand-side management is an essential complement to supply-side grid investments for protecting communities during the most dangerous heat events.

Case Study Title: Washington uses proactive drought declarations to manage heat-driven water stress before it becomes a crisis
Description: Rising temperatures are reducing snowpack across the American West, threatening the water supplies that communities, farms, and ecosystems depend on. Washington has used proactive drought declarations tied to dismal snowpack conditions to activate state resources and coordinate water management responses before shortages become crises. By treating low snowpack as a drought emergency, Washington can support impacted communities earlier in the season. This anticipatory approach to heat-driven drought illustrates how heat policy must extend beyond summer cooling to year-round management of a warming climate's effects on water, agriculture, and ecosystems.

Case Study Title: North Dakota restricts heavy truck travel on highways during extreme temperatures
Description: Extreme heat damages road infrastructure by softening asphalt. Heavy loads in particular wear down on road infrastructure and create safety hazards and costly repairs. North Dakota's highway temperature restrictions limit the travel of large loads on state highways when temperatures exceed 90 degrees Fahrenheit, protecting road surfaces during the most vulnerable periods. This demonstrates that heat has an impact on physical infrastructure that requires proactive management, and that governments that build heat thresholds into their standard operating procedures can prevent damage and expensive remediation. North Dakota's road restrictions are a practical model for integrating heat into infrastructure management.

Case Study Title: Massachusetts creates a dedicated disaster relief fund with heat as an eligible trigger
Description: A series of devastating weather events in 2023, including floods, frosts, and extreme heat, galvanized the Massachusetts state legislature to act. The Disaster Relief and Resiliency Fund, created as part of the fiscal year 2025 state budget, establishes a standing fund administered by the Massachusetts Emergency Management Agency to respond to natural and human-caused disasters, explicitly including extreme heat. The fund is designed to provide rapid relief to municipalities without waiting for a federal declaration. The creation of the fund reflects a growing recognition that extreme heat is not just a public health concern but a disaster preparedness one, and that states need flexible, pre-positioned resources to respond quickly when heat emergencies strike.

Description: Local emergency declarations give city and county governments the authority to rapidly mobilize resources, coordinate with state agencies, and communicate urgency to the public. Boston, New Orleans, and Multnomah County have each issued local heat emergency declarations, triggering the activation of cooling centers, public communications campaigns, and coordination with social service agencies. Local declarations allow the governments closest to the affected population to act without waiting for state authorization. Regular use of local declarations also normalizes heat as the serious emergency it is.

Case Study Title: Local jurisdictions in Maricopa County operate overnight heat relief centers to protect residents when nighttime temperatures stay dangerous
Description: Daytime cooling centers close when the risk is not over. In Phoenix and surrounding communities, nighttime temperatures regularly remain above 90 degrees, making sleep dangerous for people without air conditioning or who are homeless. Local jurisdictions in Maricopa County, including Phoenix and Chandler, operate overnight heat relief centers that remain open through the night, giving residents a safe place to sleep during the most extreme heat events. These centers require more staffing than daytime-only options, but they address a gap that daytime resources alone simply cannot fill. The overnight center model is an important innovation for any jurisdiction where nighttime temperatures pose a meaningful risk.

Case Study Title: Jacksonville offers free transit during heat waves to connect residents to cooling
Description: Getting to a cooling center is not always easy, especially for older adults, people with disabilities, and low-income residents who may lack access to a car. Jacksonville's Stay Cool JAX program provides free transit rides during heat emergencies so that residents can reach cooling centers, splash pads, libraries, and other cool spaces without financial or logistical barriers. Jacksonville's model is replicable in any city with a transit system and demonstrates how combining multiple services, cooling centers and free transit, creates a more accessible and effective heat response.

Case Study Title: New York City Council passes legislation to codify cooling centers into law
Description: Cooling centers availability has historically depended on ad hoc decisions by city officials during emergencies. The New York City Council passed Intro 998-A, which would codify cooling center requirements into law, specifying when centers must open, how many must be available, and how the city must communicate their locations to residents. The legislation has not yet been signed by the mayor. If enacted, it would transform cooling centers from a discretionary service into a legal entitlement, creating accountability structures that community members could use to hold the city to its obligations. The bill's progress reflects growing recognition that consistent, predictable access to cooling is a matter of public safety, not administrative convenience.

Case Study Title: Metro Portland declares a regionwide heat season to sustain readiness from May through September
Description: Heat emergencies do not arrive on a predictable schedule, so governments that only prepare reactively are always a step behind. Metro, the regional government for the Portland area, has addressed this by declaring a regionwide heat season running from May 1st through September 30th. The declaration keeps partner agencies, community organizations, and the public in a sustained state of readiness throughout the months when dangerous heat is possible, rather than scrambling to activate resources only after a heat wave is forecast. Metro's approach ensures that cooling centers are identified and staffed, communication channels are tested, and vulnerable residents are connected to resources before the heat arrives, not during it.

Case Study Title: California funds community heat resilience projects through a dedicated grant program
Description: Reducing heat risk over the long term requires capital investments in shade, green infrastructure, cooling centers, and grid resilience which are difficult for communities to finance without dedicated funding. California's Extreme Heat Community Resilience Program provides grants to local governments and community organizations to build heat resilience infrastructure, with an explicit priority of communities that face the greatest risk and the least capacity to self-fund improvements. By creating a dedicated, heat-only funding stream, California has been able to build a pipeline of local projects that would not otherwise have been financed.

Case Study Title: Illinois invests in battery storage and virtual power plants to prepare its grid for extreme heat
Description: Extreme heat drives peak electricity demand at the same time it strains grid infrastructure, creating dangerous conditions when the grid is most needed. Illinois's Clean and Reliable Grid Affordability Act (CRGA) directly targets these risks through a suite of provisions designed to reduce peak demand and expand grid capacity. The law mandates 3 gigawatts of new battery storage, creates a "Storage for All" program bringing backup power benefits to income-eligible households and public facilities, and establishes a new virtual power plant program projected to add 3.6 gigawatts of additional grid capacity by 2035 through aggregated distributed resources. CRGA also strengthens energy efficiency programs and introduces opt-in time-of-use rates to reduce demand during peak heat hours. The law demonstrates how grid modernization investments can simultaneously protect reliability, reduce costs, and build resilience against the heat-driven demand spikes that put the grid at greatest risk.
Link Title: Clean and Reliable Grid Affordability Act (CRGA)

Case Study Title: Texas and California's rapidly growing battery storage fleet is strengthening grid resilience during extreme heat
Description: Texas and California have faced repeated extreme heat events that push electricity demand to record levels. In response, California has made major investments in battery storage, building one of the largest utility-scale storage fleets in the world, enabling grid operators to dispatch stored energy during evening peak demand hours when solar generation drops but cooling loads remain high. During several recent heat emergencies, California's storage fleet helped prevent rotating outages. Texas has also rapidly expanded battery storage and solar capacity, moving toward a more distributed, flexible grid that is better able to manage the demand spikes that accompany summer heat. These efforts demonstrate that large-scale battery investment is not just a clean energy strategy; it is a heat resilience strategy, providing the flexible grid capacity needed to air conditioning on when temperatures are at their most dangerous.

Case Study Title: Texas creates a program to invest in back-up power
Description: ERCOT, Texas's electric grid, has faced intense scrutiny over reliability during extreme weather events, and extreme heat is a recurring stress test. The state's Back-Up Power Program funds microgrids and backup power systems at critical infrastructure sites to ensure hospitals, emergency shelters, and water treatment facilities can remain operational during grid failures. This investment reflects a recognition that grid resilience during extreme heat requires both distributed backup capacity and a modernized main grid capable of meeting soaring demand.

Case Study Title: Rhode Island's Infrastructure Bank finances resilience projects through an innovative revolving fund
Description: Public grants alone cannot finance the scale of heat resilience infrastructure that communities need. Rhode Island's Resilient Rhody program, administered through the Rhode Island Infrastructure Bank, uses a revolving loan fund model to finance climate resilience projects, including those that reduce heat risk, at below-market rates. Loan repayments from completed projects recapitalize the fund, allowing sustainability and not requiring continuous new appropriations. This model is particularly valuable for smaller municipalities that cannot access capital markets on favorable terms on their own. Rhode Island's Infrastructure Bank demonstrates how states can create durable, self-sustaining financing mechanisms for heat resilience investment.

Case Study Title: North Carolina upgrades air conditioning in correctional facilities to protect incarcerated people from extreme heat
Description: Incarcerated people are among the most heat-vulnerable populations in the country, confined in facilities they cannot leave. North Carolina's Department of Adult Correction has undertaken air conditioning upgrades at correctional facilities to bring safe temperatures to more of the people in its custody. These investments reflect a duty-of-care obligation: the state is responsible for the health and safety of people it incarcerates. North Carolina's effort is a model for other states with aging correctional infrastructure, and a reminder that heat policy must account for all populations, including those who have the least power to advocate for themselves.

Hawaii's green fee model is an innovative financing mechanism that other tourism-dependent states and communities can adapt.

Massachusetts’ model creates a more equitable funding model while reducing reliance on general fund appropriations.

Description: Local governments struggle to finance the long-term infrastructure investments needed to reduce heat risk because they lack access to dedicated revenue streams. California's Climate Resilience Districts legislation enables the creation of special financing districts that can issue bonds backed by tax increment revenue to fund climate resilience infrastructure like heat mitigation projects such as shade, green infrastructure, and cooling facilities. California gives cities and counties a new tool to invest in heat safety without relying entirely on state or federal grants. Climate resilience districts can target investment in the areas most at risk and in most need.

Description: New buildings constructed today will face decades of rising heat, making building codes and zoning requirements a powerful lever for embedding heat resilience into the built environment. Developments that score well receive zoning benefits; those that score poorly must make improvements. Cambridge ensures that new construction contributes to cooling the city rather than adding to its heat burden, and that the cost of heat resilience is shared by those who profit from new development.

Case Study Title: New Orleans builds a city-wide virtual power plant to improve grid resilience and reduce energy costs
Description: New Orleans faces an aging grid, frequent extreme weather, and some of the highest electricity bills in the country. In response, the city has launched an effort to build a city-wide virtual power plant, with settlement money from its investor-owned utility. The virtual power plant will aggregate distributed solar, battery storage, and demand response assets across households and businesses. When activated, the virtual power plant can reduce strain on the grid during peak demand, lower electricity costs for participants, and supply backup power to critical facilities during outages. New Orleans' approach shows how cities can use distributed energy resources to simultaneously reduce energy costs for low-income households and build resilience against the combined risks of extreme heat and power system failure.

Case Study Title: Boise's City of Trees Challenge invests in urban tree canopy to reduce heat and build community pride
Description: Urban trees are an effective long-term tool for reducing extreme heat, yet many cities have seen their tree canopy decline as development removes mature trees faster than new ones can grow. Boise's City of Trees Challenge combines a citywide tree planting initiative with an annual municipal investment of $80,000 dedicated to expanding and maintaining Boise's urban forest. The program treats tree canopy as core public infrastructure with a maintenance budget, rather than a discretionary beautification project. By setting a measurable canopy goal and committing recurring funding to achieve it, Boise ensures that its urban forest grows over time rather than being planted episodically during campaigns and then neglected.

Salt Lake City's Green Loop is a major planned development corridor that integrates trees, shade structures, green infrastructure, and pedestrian-friendly design to reduce urban heat island effects along a key stretch of the city. By treating the Green Loop as a heat resilience project, not just a parks or transportation project, Salt Lake City is demonstrating how capital planning decisions can build cooling into the city's core infrastructure. The Green Loop will benefit residents, commuters, and businesses for decades, making it one of the most durable heat investments the city can make.

Tucson's Storm to Shade program uses stormwater fees to invest in green infrastructure, including bioswales, rain gardens, and planted basins. The program delivers a double dividend: reducing the burden on Tucson's stormwater system while building passive cooling capacity across the city. Storm to Shade also prioritizes neighborhoods with the least existing shade and tree canopy, ensuring that infrastructure investment reaches the residents who need it most.

Case Study Title: Cities set formal tree canopy targets to hold themselves accountable for long-term cooling investment
Description: Planting trees is easy; sustaining the political commitment and funding to actually expand urban canopy over decades is hard. Cities including New York City, Chicago, Washington D.C., Baltimore, Austin, Fort Worth, and San Francisco have set formal, publicly stated targets for tree canopy coverage, creating accountability structures that outlast any individual administration. Targets give advocates a benchmark to hold governments to, give budget offices a justification for recurring funding, and give residents a way to track progress. Without explicit targets, trees are planted without the investment of resources to maintain them. With them, cities build the long-term institutional commitment needed to realize canopy's full heat mitigation potential.

By grounding infrastructure investment in evidence about heat risk, Boston can ensure that limited public dollars reach the neighborhoods with the greatest need.
